name: Gray Dogwood
summary: Disease-resistant native shrub for wet edges, open margins, and disturbed areas. White berries on striking red stems persist into fall — one of the most visually distinctive shrubs in the native palette. More than 40 bird species consume the berries. Spreads aggressively by root suckers.
notes: Best for wet edges, stream margins, and open disturbed areas where other shrubs struggle. Tolerates wet soil, clay, poor drainage. Spreads by root suckering to form dense colonies — gives excellent nesting cover. No disease concerns (unlike flowering dogwood). Natural arrival in open areas.

planting.soil: Wet, clay, compacted — the worst drainage spots
planting.site_notes: Plant in wet margins, ditches, and areas with poor drainage where other species fail. Spreads aggressively by root suckers into dense nesting cover. No deer protection needed. White berries on striking red stems.
